evidence_quotes.quote: there are video games that are designed to be destroyed and removed from their owners libraries without their knowledge or consent when the distributing company believes it is no longer profitable to support them.
End-of-Life plan should be in place to ensure that art is not destroyed in short-sighted business decisions.

paragraphs.text: Dear sir/madame, I am writing to convey the opportunity that exists for this new act to extend its protections across the digital media space to encompass video games. Right now, there are video games that are designed to be destroyed and removed from their owners libraries without their knowledge or consent when the distributing company believes it is no longer profitable to support them. This blatant destruction of art recalls the disregard that early films were treated with that led to much of the motion picture medium before the 1930's to be lost from history. From a consumer right's perspective, these products are being removed from owner's libraries without recourse and the money spent on them is not refunded, I believe that this is wrong and goes against the values of fair business that the EU is founded upon. The Digital Fairness act should be extended to provide protections for digital art, especially video games, so that they cannot be destroyed at a whim. Such protections should include stipulations that games should be accessible in at least some form after support for them has ended, an End-of-Life plan should be in place to ensure that art is not destroyed in short-sighted business decisions. A European Citizen's Initiative petition exists called "Stop Destroying Videogames" with over 1 million signatures calling for such a law to be implemented. Art should be protected for future generations, it is a critical responsibility for us to do so. Thank you very much for reading this, Connor Duignan, Saoránach na hEireann
